questions.question: Explain the difference between Protection and Security in operating systems with examples.
keyPoints: Protection is internal mechanism controlling access by authorized users/processes
Security is external defense against unauthorized access/threats
Example of Protection: file permissions, process isolation
Example of Security: firewalls, antivirus, encryption against external attacks

questions.question: Describe the Access Matrix model for protection. How is it implemented in practical systems?
keyPoints: Access Matrix has subjects as rows and objects as columns
Each cell contains access rights for that subject-object pair
Implemented as Access Control Lists (columns) or Capability Lists (rows)
Used in systems like UNIX permissions, Windows ACLs

questions.question: Explain the Principle of Least Privilege and its importance in operating system security.
keyPoints: Users/processes should have minimum privileges needed to complete tasks
Reduces potential damage from errors or malicious actions
Limits spread of malware and unauthorized access
Example: root access only when absolutely necessary

questions.question: Differentiate between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC).
keyPoints: DAC: owner has discretionary control over resource access
MAC: system enforces access based on classification levels
DAC used in UNIX/Windows, MAC used in military/government systems
DAC allows users to modify permissions, MAC does not

questions.question: What is Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)? Explain its advantages over traditional access control models.
keyPoints: RBAC assigns permissions to roles, users assigned to roles
Simplifies administration in large organizations
Easier to modify - change role permissions affects all users in role
More closely matches organizational structure
Reduces complexity of managing individual user permissions

questions.question: Discuss various types of security threats (viruses, worms, Trojan horses) in operating systems.
keyPoints: Virus: self-replicating, attaches to programs, requires host execution
Worm: self-replicating, spreads across networks without host
Trojan Horse: disguised as legitimate software, contains malicious code
All can cause data theft, system damage, unauthorized access

questions.question: Explain the concept of Authentication in operating systems. Discuss common authentication methods.
keyPoints: Authentication verifies identity before granting access
Password-based: knowledge factor
Biometric: physical characteristic factor (fingerprint, iris)
Token-based: possession factor (smart card, security token)
Multi-factor authentication combines two or more methods

questions.question: Discuss in detail the various access control models used in operating systems. Compare their features, advantages, and limitations.
keyPoints: DAC: owner-controlled, flexible but less secure, used in general computing
MAC: system-enforced, high security, inflexible, used in military/government
RBAC: role-based, efficient administration, scalable for organizations
Capability-based: tokens carried by subjects, supports delegation
Access Matrix: theoretical model, implemented via ACLs or capabilities
Comparison: security level, flexibility, administration overhead

questions.question: Explain the security mechanisms and features that an operating system should provide. Discuss various security threats and the measures to counter them.
keyPoints: Authentication: verify user identity
Authorization: determine access rights after authentication
Encryption: protect data confidentiality
Auditing: track system activities for accountability
Firewall: filter network traffic
Antivirus: detect and remove malicious software
Intrusion Detection Systems: detect suspicious activities
Security threats: viruses, worms, trojans, DoS attacks, phishing
Countermeasures: regular updates, strong passwords, access controls, backup

questions.question: Describe the concept of a trusted operating system. What are the criteria for a system to be considered trusted? Discuss the security kernel and its role.
keyPoints: Trusted system meets defined security requirements
Criteria: secure design, verification of security policy, trusted path, audit capabilities
Security kernel: core trusted component mediating all access requests
Implements reference monitor concept
Must be tamper-proof and always invoked
Provides assurance of correct security enforcement

questions.question: What is the Access Control List (ACL) mechanism? How is it implemented in modern operating systems? Discuss its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: ACL attached to each object (file/device) lists subjects and their rights
Windows uses ACL extensively for file and folder permissions
UNIX uses simplified ACL with owner/group/others plus extended ACLs
Advantages: simple to understand, object-centric view, supports fine-grained control
Disadvantages: can grow large for popular files, permission changes require modifying object
Comparison with capabilities: ACL is object-oriented, capability is subject-oriented
